# Cardiovascular disease & hypertension

> The cascade reaches the heart and the arteries. The same insulin-resistant, dyslipidaemic biology that ran
> through obesity and diabetes now shows up as raised blood pressure and furring arteries, the leading cause
> of death in Singapore, and much of it modifiable by diet. What matters here is recognising the dietary
> levers that lower blood pressure and cardiovascular risk, and knowing when to refer or co-manage.

## Cardiovascular disease & hypertension at a glance

This chapter follows the *Type 2 Diabetes* chapter in the cardiometabolic cascade and hands the renal end
forward to the *Chronic Kidney Disease* chapter. Two facts set the stakes: cardiovascular disease causes
**about a third of all deaths** in Singapore, and **roughly one in three adults has hypertension, over half
of them undiagnosed** [@shfHeartStats2024; @nphs2024]. This is population-scale primary prevention,
delivered lifestyle-first.

Three habits of mind run through the chapter:

- **Think in whole dietary *patterns*, not single nutrients**, the modern framing for heart-healthy eating [@aha2021diet].
- **Recognise the levers, refer for the plan.** A dietitian delivers the structured change, and drug decisions belong to the clinician.
- **You do not memorise blood-pressure or cholesterol *targets*.** They are risk-tiered and *looked up*; knowing they exist and are individualised is enough.

## The science underneath: how diet reaches the artery and the blood pressure

Why does what you eat move a blood-pressure reading and fur an artery? Two pathways do most of the work, and
they converge on the vessel wall. The underlying biology, how fats are carried as lipoproteins and how the
body handles sodium and water, belongs to the *Macronutrients* and the *Micronutrients, Hydration &
Electrolytes* chapters; here we follow how diet pushes on them.

![Two dietary pathways converge on the artery: saturated and trans fat raise LDL cholesterol, which builds atherosclerotic plaque in the artery wall; dietary sodium raises blood volume and vascular tone, raising blood pressure (dietary potassium counter-regulates). Both damage the vessel and drive heart attack and stroke.](/figures/mechanisms/cvd-diet-pathways.svg){#fig-cvd-pathways width="90%" fig-align="center"}

**The artery.** Dietary **saturated and *trans* fat raise LDL cholesterol** (the "bad" cholesterol carried in
low-density lipoproteins) [@mensink2003]. LDL is deposited in, and inflames, the artery wall, building the
**atherosclerotic plaque** that narrows vessels and ruptures to cause heart attacks and strokes. Improving fat
*quality*, the lever below, therefore measurably cuts cardiovascular events [@hooper2020; @sacks2017].

**The pressure.** Dietary **sodium** expands blood volume and raises vascular tone, pushing blood pressure up
in a graded, dose-dependent way [@he2013saltbp; @huang2020sodiumdose]. Dietary **potassium** counter-regulates,
helping the kidney shed sodium and relaxing vessels [@who2012potassium]. Blood pressure and plaque then act on
the same endothelium — the inner lining of the vessel — and the upstream insulin resistance and dyslipidaemia
carried over from the *Obesity & Metabolic Syndrome* and *Type 2 Diabetes* chapters feed both.

## Think patterns, not single nutrients

The modern framing is a set of whole-pattern features rather than a list of good and bad nutrients. The
American Heart Association organises heart-healthy eating around **plenty of fruit, vegetables,
wholegrains and legumes; fish and plant proteins; liquid plant oils over tropical or hydrogenated fats;
minimally processed over ultra-processed; little added sugar or salt** [@aha2021diet]. The WHO adds a few
population targets: **salt under 5 g/day, saturated fat under 10% of energy, *trans* fat under 1%, free
sugars under 10%, and at least 400 g of fruit and vegetables a day** [@who2020diet]. Everything below (DASH,
sodium, fat quality, the Mediterranean pattern) hangs off this umbrella, and Singapore's **My Healthy Plate**
is its no-arithmetic local form [@hpbHealthyPlate].

## Lowering the blood pressure

Start with the pressure pathway. Two levers push on blood pressure: the DASH pattern, and the sodium–potassium balance.

### DASH: the blood-pressure pattern

The **DASH** pattern (rich in fruit, vegetables, wholegrains and low-fat dairy) was designed to lower blood
pressure, and it does so **in its own right**: in the landmark feeding trial it cut blood pressure by about
**5.5/3.0 mmHg overall and 11.4/5.5 mmHg in people with hypertension**, with sodium and weight held constant —
so the *pattern itself* did the work [@appel1997dash]. Two qualifiers travel with it. That was a short controlled-feeding
study with a **surrogate (blood-pressure) endpoint**, and the free-living effect people achieve on their own
is smaller, with pooled trials landing nearer **3–7/2–4 mmHg** [@filippou2020dashmeta; @appel2003premier]. Even so,
a few mmHg across a population meaningfully lowers stroke and heart disease.

DASH works through the **whole-food matrix** (potassium, magnesium and fibre together), which beats the
matched supplements, so the advice is *potassium-rich produce, not pills* [@alsolaiman2010dashmechanism]. And
it **stacks with cutting salt**: in DASH-Sodium, each lever helped on its own and the two together were best
of all [@sacks2001dashsodium]. In Singapore, DASH *is* My Healthy Plate: half the plate fruit and vegetables,
a quarter wholegrains, a quarter lean protein.

### Sodium down, potassium up

Sodium is Singapore's headline dietary problem. About **9 in 10 residents exceed the limit**, averaging
**~3,600 mg a day — roughly twice the WHO ceiling** — with most of it hidden in **soup bases, sauces,
seasonings and food eaten out** rather than the salt shaker [@hpb2023sodium]. The blood-pressure benefit of cutting
back is graded and reversible [@he2013saltbp], and the counterpart lever is eating **more potassium** (fruit,
vegetables, legumes).

One evidence-based option with **hard-outcome** support is a **potassium-enriched salt substitute**, which in
a large trial cut strokes and deaths [@neal2021ssass]. It carries a genuine safety caveat, which makes it a
useful do-no-harm example.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Check the kidneys before a potassium salt substitute
Potassium-based salt substitutes are helpful for *most* people, but the trial that proved they work
**excluded people with serious kidney disease and those on potassium-raising drugs** (ACE inhibitors, ARBs,
potassium-sparing diuretics), in whom extra potassium can cause dangerous **hyperkalaemia** (a high blood
potassium that can disturb heart rhythm). So before recommending one, check for kidney disease and those
medications, and if in doubt, don't. The renal potassium story is owned by the *Chronic Kidney Disease*
chapter [@neal2021ssass].
:::

(One myth to retire: the "sodium J-curve" suggesting very low salt is harmful is largely a **measurement
artefact**, and in any case irrelevant at Singapore's high average intake, where essentially everyone benefits
from cutting down [@cappuccio2022sodiummyths].)

::: {.callout-tip}
## 🍜 Eating out
The salt is in the **soup, the gravy and the sauces**, not mainly the shaker: a bowl of fishball noodle *soup*
can carry most of a day's sodium. Practical, recognise-and-advise swaps: **leave most of the soup**, ask for
**"less salt"**, take **sauce/gravy on the side**, skip the extra soy and chilli, choose steamed over deep-fried,
and add vegetables and fruit for potassium. Nationally, watch for the **"Lower in Sodium" Healthier Choice
Symbol**, and note that Nutri-Grade is being **extended beyond drinks to sauces, seasonings and instant noodles**
[@hpb2023sodium; @moh2025nutrigrade].
:::

## Protecting the artery

Now the artery wall. Its levers are about fat *quality* and the whole pattern, not eating less fat.

### Fat quality, not quantity: "replaced with what?"

The single most useful idea about dietary fat and the heart is that it is about **quality and replacement**
rather than "eat less fat." Swapping **saturated and *trans* fat for unsaturated** fat lowers LDL cholesterol and cuts
cardiovascular events: around **17% fewer events** for reducing saturated fat, and roughly a **statin-sized
30%** when saturated fat is specifically replaced with polyunsaturated fat [@mensink2003; @hooper2020;
@sacks2017]. The famous "saturated fat has been exonerated" headlines came from studies that **didn't say what
the fat was replaced with**, and when the replacement is *refined carbohydrate and sugar*, there is no
benefit [@siritarino2010]. So the question to ask of every swap is **"replaced with what?"**: butter or palm
oil for olive or canola oil, yes; butter for white bread, no.

<div data-island="fat-swap" data-src="/diagrams/fat-swap.yml"></div>

### Fish, capsule, or drug?

A common patient question is whether to buy fish-oil capsules "for the heart." The answer draws a sharp
line between three different things:

- **Oily fish is a food to recommend**, part of the pattern.
- **Routine omega-3 *supplements* failed** to prevent cardiovascular events in large trials [@manson2019vital; @ascend2018omega3]. Recommend the fish, not the capsule.
- **Icosapent ethyl** — the "fish-oil trial" people cite — is a **high-dose prescription *drug*** used in selected patients [@bhatt2019reduceit]; it is a specialist decision, and you cannot extrapolate from it to over-the-counter fish oil.

![Oily fish such as salmon is a food to recommend for heart health, though that is not the same as an over-the-counter fish-oil capsule.](/figures/food/grilled-salmon.jpg){#fig-oily-fish width="55%" fig-align="center"}

### The Mediterranean pattern

The **Mediterranean** pattern (olive oil, nuts, fish, vegetables, legumes, wholegrains) is the one with
event-level trial evidence. In PREDIMED, it lowered a composite of cardiovascular events by about **30% in
relative terms** in a high-risk Spanish population, a signal **driven mainly by stroke** [@estruch2018predimed].
Two caveats travel with it, and both show something useful about reading evidence. The effect
is a *relative* reduction on a composite endpoint in a high-risk group (not a promise of a 30% cut for
everyone), and the original 2013 paper was **retracted and then republished in 2018** after randomisation
irregularities, with its conclusion upheld: science self-correcting, not a diet debunked. Guidelines endorse
the *pattern*, not a magic number [@esc2021prevention; @medUmbrella]. The everyday translation is, again, My
Healthy Plate: plants, wholegrains, fish, and plant oils.

## Alcohol is not cardioprotective

The old reassurance that "a daily glass protects your heart" has not held up. The apparent benefit came from
comparing drinkers with an "abstainer" group that quietly included people who had stopped because they were
ill; studies using genetics to remove that bias find **risk rising with intake and no protective threshold**
[@biddinger2022mralcohol], and health bodies now state there is **no safe level** [@who2023nosafelevel]. What
*is* real is that **cutting down lowers blood pressure** (dose-dependently, most in heavy drinkers) and
triglycerides [@roerecke2017bp], so alcohol reduction is a genuine cardiovascular lever, but **never a reason
to start**. Singapore's low-risk guidance caps intake at about **two standard drinks a day for men and one for
women** (a standard drink ≈ 10 g), a harm-reduction ceiling rather than a "safe" target [@mohAlcoholLimits2018].

## The Singapore picture

**The burden and the silence.** Cardiovascular disease is Singapore's leading killer, about **30% of deaths**
[@shfHeartStats2024], and hypertension is a silent driver: measured prevalence is **~34% of adults**, but
**about half are undiagnosed** and most known cases are not well controlled [@nphs2024]. That gap between
self-reported and *measured* hypertension is the argument for screening a symptomless disease.

**The levers, made local.** Sodium is the priority (above), tackled by national machinery: a 15%-by-2026
reduction target, reduced-sodium ingredient subsidies, and the Nutri-Grade extension to sauces and instant
noodles [@hpb2023sodium; @moh2025nutrigrade]. On fats, **cooking oil is the single largest source** of
saturated fat locally, so the home lever is the *type of oil* and less deep-frying [@moh2025nutrigrade]. And
**My Healthy Plate** is the ready-made local form of the DASH/Mediterranean pattern [@hpbHealthyPlate].

**Risk is estimated, and the tool must fit the population.** Singapore uses **SG-FRS-2023**, recalibrated on
local multi-ethnic data because imported US equations mis-estimate risk across Chinese, Malay and Indian
groups — though even it estimates 10-year *coronary* risk, not stroke or total cardiovascular risk
[@sgfrs2023]. National **ACE guidances** for hypertension and lipids are risk-tiered and lifestyle-first, with
drug targets and classes **signposted, not drilled** [@aceHtn2023; @aceLipid2023]; structured dietary change is
referred through **EatWise SG** [@mohEatWiseSG].

::: {.callout-important}
## You look the targets up, you don't memorise them
There is no cholesterol or blood-pressure number to recite here. Targets are **risk-tiered**: a clinician
estimates cardiovascular risk (in Singapore, with SG-FRS-2023) and the ACE guidance generates the individual's
target. At this stage, recognise that they exist, are individualised, and are looked up, then
deliver the lifestyle levers and refer, rather than applying drug thresholds from memory [@aceHtn2023; @aceLipid2023].
:::

## What does a heart-healthy plate look like?

It is a *pattern*, not a nutrient scorecard: plenty of vegetables, fruit, wholegrains and legumes; fish and
plant proteins; **unsaturated oils in place of saturated and trans fat** ("replaced with what?"); **salt down
and potassium up**; the *fish*, not the capsule; and alcohol never taken up for the heart. In Singapore that
is **My Healthy Plate** at the hawker stall: less soup and gravy, brown rice, more vegetables, a *teh-o
kosong*. Recognise these levers, refer for the structured plan and the risk-tiered targets, and the cascade
runs on to the kidneys, where the same sodium and potassium levers acquire new cautions.

## Check your understanding

<div data-island="quiz" data-src="/quizzes/cardiovascular-disease-hypertension.quiz.yml"></div>

## Practise: Mr Chua and the hawker-lunch heart

Mr Chua is 52, eats most meals at the hawker centre, and comes in with a blood pressure of 148/92 (repeated at
146/90) and a raised LDL cholesterol. He has no previous heart disease, doesn't smoke, and isn't diabetic. His
first question is "Do I need medication, doctor?", and later he mentions a potassium salt substitute he saw
online, and asks whether a daily beer is good for his heart. Work through the visit: lead with the right
first move, translate the pattern to his hawker plate, handle the salt-substitute safety check, and notice
where memorising a target, or reaching for a drug first, would be the wrong reflex. Your choices branch the
case.

<div data-island="case" data-src="/cases/mr-chua-cvd.case.yml"></div>
